title: Ajahn Funn: That is why we have come to remind our minds to meditate, to choose and refine our mind. If we do not choose…
content:
Choosing and Refining the Mind: Like Taking the Flesh of a Fish

That is why we have come to remind our minds to meditate, to choose and refine our mind. If we do not choose and refine it, our mind cannot be used. Our mind has both good and bad. We must choose and refine it, just like a fish: it has both benefit and harm. A wise person takes only the flesh – it is easy. A foolish person eats the bones and gets them stuck in the throat and dies. Our mind has everything: petas, hell beings, the deaf, the blind, the mute, the insane, the deranged – all are there. Also good ears, good eyes, beauty – that is there. The mind of a king, a millionaire, a minister – all are there. Deva sons, devas, Sakka, Brahmās – all are there. Choose which one to take. If asked: how can we know the mind of a deva son, deva, Sakka, Brahmā? It is buddho, buddho. Our mind is joyful, our mind is bright, our mind is clear – that leads in this direction. Now the dark ones: our mind is defiled, the mind is not clear, the mind is dark and murky, chaotic, troubled. When suffering mind arises, it leads to an unhappy destiny. The mind that is happy and at ease leads to a happy destiny. This is the practice.

title: Ajahn Funn: Thus, the Buddha taught us to have these three duties, to train and cultivate our character. Now, beauty is not the beauty of…
content:
Beauty in the Beginning, Middle, and End: Virtue, Concentration, Wisdom

Thus, the Buddha taught us to have these three duties, to train and cultivate our character. Now, beauty is not the beauty of necklaces and garlands. In the Pali, it is said: ādikalyāṇaṃ majjhekalyāṇaṃ pariyosānakalyāṇaṃ – beautiful in the beginning, beautiful in the middle, beautiful in the end. This is true beauty. Beauty in the beginning means having virtue, i.e., restraining body, speech, and mind properly, not committing any fault great or small with body or mind, keeping the mind normal, seeing the normality of our body and mind. When we have normal, proper virtue, then majjhekalyāṇaṃ – beauty in the middle is concentration (samādhi). With concentration, the mind becomes firmly established, the mind is steady and upright, the mind is cool and calm, the mind is happy and at ease. That is samādhi. When it is established, it is steady. People today suffer because they are not steady. Aniccaṃ means unsteady. The Buddha taught us to make the mind steady like Nibbāna, which is permanent, not changing or shifting, free from everything. Because we are unsteady, we are like this. Whatever is unsteady, that is suffering. Whatever is suffering, that is not self. Therefore, he taught us to make the mind steady.

title: Ajahn Funn: Buddho means the knower. Pay homage every day. After paying homage, wherever you go, keep it in mind. You must go to the…
content:
Examining the Mind Daily: Like Lighting a Lamp in the Darkness

Buddho means the knower. Pay homage every day. After paying homage, wherever you go, keep it in mind. You must go to the temple and observe your mind: focus and see “Buddho, Buddho.” Examine it every single day. If you don’t examine it – missing a day or two – it won’t do. You must examine it constantly. When sitting, examine; when lying down, examine; when standing, examine; when walking, examine. Why examine? So that you know. When you examine, you see: “Ah, our mind is good. Sitting is good, standing is good, walking is good, going anywhere is good – no danger, no enemies, no evil, no harm.” That is good. If we don’t examine, we don’t know. It’s dark like the night – we can’t see ourselves, can we? Others can’t see us; the trees, the ground – we can’t see them; it’s pitch black. But if we examine, it’s like lighting a lamp, or like daytime. We see ourselves, see other people, see all living beings; we see our own life; we see our own suffering and happiness. We were born and we love our life, love ourselves, love what is ours. If that is so, when others harm us – do we feel good? No, we don’t. And others – when we harm them, do they feel good? They don’t either.

Since both sides don’t feel good, we should not harm each other. Likewise, if a thief steals from us, we don’t feel good; if we steal from him, he doesn’t feel good. If he kills us, we don’t feel good; if we kill him, he doesn’t feel good. Since everyone loves their own life like this, then we should not harm one another.

title: Ajahn Funn: At this moment, our body is proper, our speech is proper. Only the mind is still wandering; it is not yet proper. See?…
content:
The Middle Is Beautiful: Samādhi – The Mind Established Within

At this moment, our body is proper, our speech is proper. Only the mind is still wandering; it is not yet proper. See? What is being proper like? It is not sending out to left or right, front or back, above or below. It stays right in the middle of our chest, right in the middle of our heart. Normal body, normal mind — that we call sīla. That is how it is. Therefore, let us all know, see, and understand. We have come to train and refine our character. We want goodness, for no other reason. This is precisely why. Majjhekalyāṇaṃ — the middle is beautiful, that is one who has samādhi. Samādhi, they say, is nothing else. They say the mind is established. Look: is our mind established or not? What is it like to be established? It is like a post standing firmly like this. As an analogy: if it stands like this, it can be relied upon. If it does not stand, it topples and breaks like this, and cannot be relied upon. Now, our mind does not stay still; it sends itself here and there, to front and back, left and right, above and below — so it cannot be relied upon. We must make it stand, meaning not sending it front, back, left, right, above, below, but letting it stand right in the middle of our chest.

title: Ajahn Funn: So come and look. Come and practice. If you’re intoxicated with your work, intoxicated with money, it won’t bring you any real benefits.…
content:
Whatever You Gain, You’ll Have to Discard It All

So come and look. Come and practice. If you’re intoxicated with your work, intoxicated with money, it won’t bring you any real benefits. Whatever you gain, you’ll have to discard it all. If you had money by the millions and billions, you’d have to discard it all. If you had money enough to fill this hall, you’d have to discard it all. Children filling the country: You’d have to discard them all. The only thing you’ll have left is kusalā dhammā, akusalā dhammā, that’s all. When you do these things, that’s what you gain. Aside from that, you don’t gain anything. If we do good, we’ll gain it. If we don’t do it, we won’t gain it.

Do you understand? You nuns: Keep practicing. Will any of you do the dhutaṅga practice of not lying down tonight? Are you up for the fight?

title: Ajahn Funn: In short: our mind is good, we have happiness and ease, coolness of heart – this is called kusala dhamma, which brings happiness,…
content:
Wholesome and Unwholesome States: Happiness and Suffering Are in the Mind

In short: our mind is good, we have happiness and ease, coolness of heart – this is called kusala dhamma, which brings happiness, ease, coolness of heart, and brings prosperity in the present and the future. Unwholesome states (akusala dhamma) – the mind is not good, the mind is suffering, troubled, agitated. This dhamma leads beings to fall into suffering and difficulty, to ruin in the present and the future. This is how it is. As for the viññāṇakkhandha – this consciousness is the knower, and it will go to take rebirth in accordance with what we have fabricated. These kammas carry us to be fashioned – no one else fashions us; the Dhamma brings it about by itself.

The Dhamma is right here. If you want to study Dhamma, to know Dhamma, come and look right here – look at rūpa, look at vedanā, look at saññā, look at saṅkhārā, look at this viññāṇa. Contemplate that rūpa. Why? Because He said we are deluded by rūpa. What is there in this rūpa that we are so gravely deluded? The Buddha therefore laid it down for us to contemplate this rūpa.

title: Ajahn Funn: For this reason, let us all sit and watch. From now on, listen to your own Dhamma. Is it wholesome Dhamma (kusala dhamma)…
content:
Wholesome Mind: Light, Soft, Joyful – Unwholesome: Ambitious, Scattered, Suffering

For this reason, let us all sit and watch. From now on, listen to your own Dhamma. Is it wholesome Dhamma (kusala dhamma) or unwholesome Dhamma (akusala dhamma)? Wholesome Dhamma means a mind that is skillful, cool, and refreshed; a mind that is light and at ease — kāya-lahutā, citta-lahutā. When the mind is light, the body is light. When our mind is concentrated, it becomes soft and gentle within — smiling within, content and joyful, not restless, compliant and easy to teach. That's how it is. It is soft and gentle (not weak), lighter than cotton wool, lighter than anything. Content and joyful, pīti arises — a fullness greater than sleeping, greater than eating. That's how it is. When the mind is calm, it is at ease, light throughout the entire body. Recognize this. This is the wholesome mind. As for the unwholesome: the mind is ambitious, scattered, not peaceful, only aching here and there, grasping at this and that as self, taking this and that as self — and that's how it becomes suffering.

title: Ajahn Funn: When we contemplate, what is it that makes a person? Is it that bone? How could a bone be a person? So know…
content:
Na: Bile, Phlegm, Blood, Sweat, Tears, Saliva, Mucus, Urine – Things to Be Discarded

When we contemplate, what is it that makes a person? Is it that bone? How could a bone be a person? So know "mo."

"Na" means "not" — what is it? We dwell relying on this namo. Let us know "na" and know "mo," then we will not become confused and agitated. What is "na"? Pittaṃ — bile. See? Where is the bile? Semhaṃ — phlegm. Pubbo — lymph. Lohitaṃ — blood. Where is it? Sedo — sweat. Medo — fat. Assu — tears. Vasā — oil. Kheḷo — saliva. Siṅghāṇikā — mucus. Lasikā — synovial fluid. Muttaṃ — urine. Are these a person or what? Are they things to throw away or things to keep? Who keeps these things? Who keeps snot? Who keeps saliva? Who keeps sweat and grime? Who keeps blood when it comes out? All are just discarded things. This is it.

When we see like this, we will abandon sakkāya-diṭṭhi (personality view), the holding that it is a self, a being, a person, an individual. Diṭṭhi and māna will not arise. We will be able to abandon rāga (passion), lobha (greed), dosa (anger), moha (delusion), avijjā (ignorance), taṇhā (craving), upādāna (clinging), bhava (becoming), and jāti (birth).

title: Ajahn Funn: If our mind is good, then everything we do is good, everything we seek is good, everything we take is good. Do you…
content:
If Our Mind Is Good, Then Everything We Do Is Good – No Doubt

If our mind is good, then everything we do is good, everything we seek is good, everything we take is good. Do you doubt this point? Look – is it good yet? If it is not good, we have not gained merit. Know this. From now on, we will not be confused. We will conduct ourselves and practice. When our mind is good, everything we do is good, everything we seek is good, our work is good – as explained. Our family is good, our siblings are good, our country and nation are good, without turmoil and distress. Whatever we say, we understand each other. Now, all of you – do you want turmoil and distress? Do you want things that are good? What is not good? To put it briefly: our mind is not good. When our mind is not good, everything we do is not good, our work is not good – as explained before. Family is not good, siblings are not good, neighbors and marketplace, even the nation, are in turmoil. All because the hearts of these people are not good.

title: Ajahn Funn: They are contained within our own body. The Suttas are the inhalation of breath; the Vinaya is the exhalation of breath; the Paramattha…
content:
The Three Piṭakas Within the Body: In-Breath, Out-Breath, and the Knower

They are contained within our own body. The Suttas are the inhalation of breath; the Vinaya is the exhalation of breath; the Paramattha Piṭaka is the knowing one inside that preserves the life faculty (jīvitindriya) so that it does not break apart. These 84,000 Dhamma aggregates are thus the Three Treasures that protect us. We must practice for ourselves. Who can make us do it? Even our parents cannot make us. It depends on the Dhamma itself. Dhammo have rakkhati dhammacāriṃ – Dhamma protects the one who lives by Dhamma. Beings depend on Dhamma, and Dhamma depends on beings. If beings practice Dhamma well, then Dhamma brings them goodness. Think about it: who makes our in-breath and out-breath? If it could be made, then hold your breath and don’t die! If the Three Treasures were to abandon us, no doctor could save us, no one could save us. When our body runs out of breath, it is like a piece of wood, a log – nothing. Therefore, we must have the Three Treasures protecting us. If we practice well, they bring us good; if we practice poorly, they bring us bad.
